query: signs of tension pneumothorx in traumatic injury including mediastinal deviation, decreased breath sounds, and hypotension

retrieved_docs.title: Tension Pneumothorax -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tension pneumothorax represents a life-threatening condition marked by intrapleural air accumulation under pressure, causing lung collapse and mediastinal shift. Causes include penetrating or blunt thoracic trauma, barotrauma from mechanical ventilation, or iatrogenic injury. A 1-way valve mechanism allows air entry during inspiration but blocks exit during expiration, escalating intrathoracic pressure. Clinical diagnosis hinges on signs like hypotension, unilateral breath sounds, distended neck veins, and tracheal deviation. Immediate management involves needle decompression followed by tube thoracostomy to restore lung expansion and alleviate hemodynamic compromise. Complications, such as cardiac arrest, recurrent tension physiology, or pulmonary injury, arise without prompt intervention. Timely recognition and treatment correlate with improved outcomes, whereas delays increase risks of significant morbidity or mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Chest Injury -- Treatment / Management -- Tension Pneumothorax
retrieved_docs.content: Signs and symptoms of tension pneumothorax include hypoxia, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, tracheal deviation away from the injured side, and diminished breath sounds, hyperresonance, hyperexpansion, and reduced chest wall movements on the injured side.

retrieved_docs.title: Tension Pneumothorax -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The combined physical findings of severe respiratory distress, hypotension, an enlarged hemithorax, ipsilaterally absent breath sounds, and contralateral tracheal deviation will distinguish tension pneumothorax from these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Chest Injury -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses to consider when evaluating a chest injury include the following: Tension pneumothorax Open pneumothorax Hemothorax Flail chest Pericardial effusion Cardiac tamponade Aortic rupture Tracheal or bronchial tree injury Myocardial contusion Pulmonary contusion Diaphragmatic rupture Esophageal rupture

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Trauma -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of tension pneumothorax is clinical—patients with tension pneumothorax present with respiratory distress, tachypnea, and hypoxia. Decreased or absent breath sounds occur on the affected side. The trachea is deviated away from the affected side. Neck veins may be distended. In patients with hypovolemia, the neck veins may not be distended. Pulmonary contusion should be suspected in patients with chest wall injury, especially if presenting with hypoxia. The degree of hypoxia often correlates with contusion size. [9] [10] The physical examination must also assess for concomitant injuries. A detailed vascular examination is warranted, including bilateral pulse and upper extremity blood pressure. Patients who present unconscious but are resuscitated and stabilized require a complete neurologic examination.

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracic Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tension pneumothorax is the presumed diagnosis when patients present with chest trauma, respiratory distress, and hypotension. A physical exam will also demonstrate specific clinical signs, such as tracheal deviation away from the affected side, decreased or absent breath sounds on the affected side, and subcutaneous emphysema on the affected side. If recognized in the field, immediate decompression using a 14-gauge needle placed in the second intercostal space in the midclavicular line is indicated. It should be noted that recent data suggests that needle decompression through the fifth intercostal space in the anterior axillary line correlates with a lower chance of failure (16.7%) due to body habitus compared to the midclavicular line placement (42.5%). [12] Once in the emergency department, patients who have undergone needle decompression in the field must then undergo immediate tube thoracostomy for definitive management.

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Trauma -- Evaluation -- Chest Radiography
retrieved_docs.content: A portable chest radiograph is required in most trauma codes (see Image. X-Ray, Blunt Chest Trauma). However, the diagnosis of tension pneumothorax is clinical. If suspected, rapid decompression must be immediately performed. Radiographic confirmation is often not necessary for intervention. However, traditional physical examination indicators like tracheal deviation and distended neck veins exhibit low sensitivity when diagnosing tension pneumothorax. Point-of-care ultrasound provides accurate identification of tension pneumothorax. [11] In supine chest radiography, small- and medium-sized pneumothoraces and hemothoraces may be missed. An aortic injury should be suspected in the presence of a widened mediastinum, apical capping, aortic knob loss, or left mainstem bronchus deviation. A ratio of over 25% of the chest width is useful in determining mediastinal widening. An occult pneumothorax is suspected if a deep costophrenic sulcus, hemidiaphragm hyperlucency, and an abnormally well-defined mediastinal or cardiac border are evident. [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In hemothorax cases, adequate drainage is imperative to prevent blood retention in the pleural space. Retained hemothorax can lead to empyema requiring video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery. [30] Most thoracic trauma cases can be managed nonoperatively. However, operative management should not be delayed when warranted. Operative thoracic injury exploration should be considered if initial (CTT) drainage exceeds 1000 to 1500 mL, approximately 200 mL per hour for 2 to 4 hours, or hypotension of unknown cause continues despite resuscitation by blood transfusion. Asymptomatic, stable individuals with an occult pneumothorax may not require a CTT. Patients with occult pneumothoraces rarely progress to tension physiology. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemothorax -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical findings of hemothorax are broad and may overlap with pneumothorax; these include respiratory distress, tachypnea, decreased or absent breath sounds, dullness to percussion, chest wall asymmetry, tracheal deviation, hypoxia, narrow pulse pressure, and hypotension. Inspect the chest wall for signs of contusion, abrasions, "seat belt sign," penetrating injury, paradoxical movement ("flail chest”), ecchymosis, deformities, crepitus, and point tenderness. Distended neck veins are concerning for pneumothorax or pericardial tamponade but might be absent in the setting of hypovolemia.  Increased respiratory rate, effort, and use of accessory muscles may be signs of impending respiratory failure. The following physical findings should prompt the clinician to consider these conditions:

retrieved_docs.title: Traumatic Aortic Injuries -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumothorax: a spontaneous or tension pneumothorax can present with sudden-onset chest pain and respiratory distress. Patients may have decreased breath sounds or tracheal deviation. A pneumothorax can be diagnosed with chest radiography or CT. Pericarditis: pericardial inflammation can cause chest pain. Patients with pericarditis may have an audible friction rub or ECG changes such as diffuse ST-segment elevation or PR depression. Pleuritis: pleural inflammation can cause sharp chest pain exacerbated by deep breathing or coughing and is commonly associated with viral infections, pneumonia, or connective tissue diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Trauma -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumothorax can occur in both blunt and penetrating thoracic trauma. A simple pneumothorax can progress to a tension pneumothorax.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pneumothorax Evaluation and Treatment -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Tension pneumothorax may present with hypotension, tachycardia, jugular venous distention, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, and respiratory distress.

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracic Trauma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The initial evaluation of a trauma patient is based on the ATLS protocol. This begins with an assessment of the patient's airway, breathing, and circulation (ABCs) during the primary survey, typically in that order. The initial evaluation of the patient who has sustained blunt or penetrating thoracic trauma is similar and geared toward the rapid identification of immediately life-threatening conditions, namely tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, aortic injury, massive hemothorax, and tracheobronchial disruption. The clinician must pay careful attention to the patient's appearance on arrival to the trauma bay. Signs of respiratory distress, agitation, diaphoresis, or unwillingness to lay flat suggest underlying cardiopulmonary injuries, such as tension pneumothorax or cardiac tamponade, in which case the breathing or circulation portions of the primary survey would need to be addressed and intervened upon first, as indicated. Intubating such patients may exacerbate the pneumothorax or hypotension and lead to cardiovascular collapse due to the increased intrathoracic pressure generated by positive pressure ventilation. Therefore, if time and personnel allow, these interventions should be performed while the patient is prepared for intubation. However, assessment of the airway is generally performed first to establish patency and evaluate the need for intubation. The assessment of breathing begins at the trachea, which is inspected and palpated to ensure that it is midline and not deviated. The chest wall in then inspected for asymmetry, auscultated for breath sounds, and palpated for tenderness, crepitus, and to detect flail segments. In assessing circulation, hypotension in the setting of thoracic trauma should raise suspicion for tension pneumothorax or tamponade, which need urgent intervention before further evaluation of the patient can proceed.

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Trauma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Tension gastrothorax Chest pain and difficulty breathing arise from gastric herniation into the thoracic cavity, leading to lung tissue compression and mediastinal shift.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Technique or Treatment -- B: Breathing and Ventilation
retrieved_docs.content: This assessment is performed first by inspection. The practitioner should look for tracheal deviation, an open pneumothorax or significant chest wounds, flail chest, paradoxical chest movement, or asymmetric chest wall excursion. Then, auscultation of both lungs should be conducted to identify decreased or asymmetric lung sounds. Decreased lung sounds can be a sign of pneumothorax or hemothorax. This, combined with either tracheal deviation or hemodynamic compromise, can signify a tension pneumothorax that should be treated with needle decompression followed by a thoracotomy tube placement. Open chest wounds should be covered immediately with a bandage taped on three sides to prevent the entry of atmospheric air into the chest. If the bandage is taped on all four sides, it may create a tension pneumothorax. If a flail chest is present and respiratory compromise, positive pressure ventilation should be provided. A flail chest may indicate an underlying pulmonary contusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Tension Pneumothorax -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumothorax may be classified as traumatic or atraumatic. Outside the hospital, traumatic pneumothorax commonly results from penetrating or blunt trauma, rib fractures, or pulmonary decompression sickness. [8] [9] Within the hospital setting, traumatic pneumothorax may develop iatrogenically as a complication of the following procedures: Central venous catheterization of the subclavian or internal jugular vein Lung biopsy Barotrauma from positive pressure ventilation Percutaneous tracheostomy Thoracentesis Pacemaker insertion Bronchoscopy Cardiopulmonary resuscitation Intercostal nerve block [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: The term “hrudník,” referring to the chest, denotes the anatomical location where pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse can develop. These three thoracic conditions are also linked with “دبيلة,” indicating an association between the clinical manifestations of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse and the entity دبيلة. Understanding that the chest (hrudník) is the site of these pathologies and recognizing their connection to دبيلة is important for accurate diagnosis and effective management of patients presenting with pleural and pulmonary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 140
retrieved_docs.content: of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” “mass of posterior mediastinum,” “mass of superior mediastinum,” “mediastinal mass,” “mediastinal mass containing calcium,” and “mediastinal effusion.” All mass‑related entities are classified as “كتلة منصفية” (mediastinal mass), whereas the effusion is classified as “اضطراب منصفي” (mediastinal disorder). Finally, the clinical syndrome “دبيلة” is associated with the triad of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, linking these respiratory complications to a specific disease entity. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how precise anatomical localization—mediastinum, chest, and thoracic subdivisions—drives the classification, imaging, and intervention of lower‑respiratory and mediastinal pathologies, supporting accurate diagnosis and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: The subdivision of thorax, identified in Arabic as دبيلة, corresponds to the pleural membrane and is directly linked to pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. These three conditions all involve the pleura: pleurisy denotes inflammation of the pleural layers, pneumothorax refers to the presence of air within the pleural space that disrupts normal lung expansion, and pulmonary collapse (atelectasis) often results from pleural pathology that prevents the lung from remaining inflated. Recognizing that the subdivision of thorax (دبيلة) is the anatomical structure underlying these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic intervention, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1186
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is categorized as a type of crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that it has the broader parent classification of crushing injury or internal injury. As a traumatic event, an internal injury of chest can act as a cause of subsequent medical complications. The occurrence of this chest injury is frequently described as being “due to” and therefore is associated with crushing mechanisms or other internal injury processes. Understanding that internal injury of chest is both a traumatic event that can be a cause of further injury and a subcategory within crushing injury or internal injury helps clinicians assess its etiology, anticipate related complications, and guide appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: The finding described as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion” is classified as an “اضطراب منصفي” (segmental disorder). This indicates that the presence of fluid accumulation in the mediastinum identified on chest CT represents a localized, segmental pathological process. Recognizing mediastinal effusion on imaging as a segmental disorder helps clinicians diagnose and manage thoracic conditions that are confined to specific anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.content: Timolol, a non‑selective beta‑adrenergic blocker, should not be used in patients who have تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ (bronchial spasm or asthma) because the drug can worsen airway constriction and precipitate respiratory distress. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse respiratory events in individuals with underlying bronchospastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2137

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 603
retrieved_docs.content: Syncope, which is classified as a type of loss of consciousness (فقدان الوعي), is clinically associated with chest constriction (خُنَاقٌ صَدْرِيّ). Chest constriction is also associated with spinal rigidity (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ). These interconnections indicate that patients experiencing syncope may report a sensation of tightness in the chest, and that spinal rigidity may be linked to the same chest symptom, underscoring the importance of evaluating both thoracic and spinal findings when assessing episodes of loss of consciousness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 974
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is an indirect procedure performed at the anatomical region referred to as hrudník (the chest). The hrudník serves as the location of several thoracic pathologies, including pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure is also linked to the Subdivision of thorax, a categorical segment of the thoracic cavity that itself has a relationship with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. Together, these connections underscore that closed chest drainage is specifically directed toward the chest area and the thoracic subdivision that are commonly affected by pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, highlighting the procedural relevance to these conditions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2872
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure are all classified as vascular conditions, and the vascular category also encompasses primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ). These entities represent distinct manifestations of abnormal pressure within the circulatory system, illustrating how various forms of hypertension and pulmonary vascular disease fall under the broader umbrella of vascular pathology. Recognizing and managing these vascular pressure abnormalities is essential for comprehensive patient care.
